---
title: "Lesson Splitting - {{Lesson Name}}"
id: lesson_splitting_{{lesson_name}}
author: brad_cannell
date: {{YYYY-MM-DD}}
date-modified: {{YYYY-MM-DD}}
audience: course_authors
category: lesson_development
tags:
  - r
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  eval: true # Switch to false to run interactively
---

# Overview

- Conversation about splitting a lesson into multiple lessons.

# Prerequisites

See [Working with Python and Chatlas](https://brad-cannell.github.io/my-ai-assistant/vignettes/python-and-chatlas.html) for setup instructions (virtual environment, package installation, API key management).

# Load Packages

```{python}
from chatlas import ChatAnthropic, ChatOpenAI
from datetime import date
from pathlib import Path
import keyring
import pickle
import os
```

# File Paths and Environment Variables

```{python}
# OpenAI Models: https://platform.openai.com/docs/models
# Anthropic Models: https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/about-claude/models/overview
# 2026-02-27: We prefer claude for lesson revisions.
model = "claude-opus-4-6"

# Today's date
today = date.today().isoformat()

# Course and lesson name
# By default, these are injected from Convert Chapters.qmd
# They can also be altered manually.
course_name = "{{Course Name}}"
chapter_name = "{{Chapter Name}}"

# Lesson file and folder paths
# By default, these are injected from Convert Chapters.qmd
# They can also be altered manually.
lesson_file_path = Path("{{lesson_file_path}}")
lesson_folder_path = lesson_file_path.parent

# Lesson content
lesson_content = (lesson_file_path).read_text(encoding="utf-8")
```

```{python}
# Check that all paths exist
paths_to_check = {
    "lesson_file_path": lesson_file_path,
    "lesson_folder_path": lesson_folder_path
}

for name, path in paths_to_check.items():
    if not path.exists():
        raise FileNotFoundError(f"Path not found — {name}: {path}")
    print(f"✓  {name}: {path}")
```

# Load helper functions

See [Python Helper Functions](https://brad-cannell.github.io/my-ai-assistant/vignettes/python-helper-functions.html) for installation instructions and a full function reference.

```{python}
from py_scripts import (
    prompt_extract_system_prompt,
    prompt_extract_user_message,
    prompt_fill_placeholders,
)
```

# Expose API

See [Storing API Keys with Keyring](https://brad-cannell.github.io/my-ai-assistant/vignettes/keyring.html) for first-time setup instructions.

```{python}
# Retrieve the password from the system keyring - Claude
claude_api = keyring.get_password("ewb-course-development-claude", "brad")

if claude_api is not None:
    # Set the retrieved value as a temporary environment variable
    os.environ['ANTHROPIC_API_KEY'] = claude_api
    print("Environment variable 'ANTHROPIC_API_KEY' set successfully within the script.")
else:
    print("Could not retrieve password from keyring.")
```

# Create Chat Object

The `system_prompt` argument is optional. Omit it for a one-off conversation, or pass a string to set a persistent role or constraints. See [System Prompts](https://brad-cannell.github.io/my-ai-assistant/vignettes/system-prompts.html) for details.

```{python}
chat = ChatAnthropic(
    model = model,
    system_prompt = "You are an expert instructional editor specializing in online and asynchronous learning. Your role is to revise course language to improve clarity, precision, tone, and readability while preserving the original instructional intent, learning objectives, and technical accuracy."
)
```

# Pass a Prompt to the AI Assistant

```{python}
user_message = f"""
I'm working on a new Epi-Workbench course called {course_name}. The course has a chapter named {chapter_name}. Currently, there is just one lesson file for this chapter. However, I want to divide it into two or more lessons. Please suggest how I should break up the content and what I should name the lessons.

A few notes about Epi-Workbench lessons:
- Lessons are Quarto (.qmd) files.
- Data used in lessons is typically simulated directly in the lesson file using a code chunk rather than loaded from an external file.
- Lesson filenames use Title Case (e.g., `Conditional Operations in R.qmd`).

Please structure your response as follows:

1. A brief rationale for how many lessons you recommend and why.
2. A summary table with one row per lesson containing: lesson number, lesson title, and filename.
3. For each lesson, a section that includes:
   - The suggested title and filename
   - A bulleted list of the content to include
   - Any simulated datasets that will need to be carried into this lesson
   - Any dependencies on content or objects introduced in a previous lesson
4. A brief rationale for each lesson explaining the pedagogical logic behind the content grouping.

Here is the lesson content:

{lesson_content}
"""
chat.chat(user_message, echo='none')
```

## Response

```{python}
#| echo: false
print(chat.get_last_turn())
```

# Follow-up and Refinement

A section for optional follow-up questions and refinements. 
- Optionally, create a fork of the chat. `import copy; chat_fork = copy.deepcopy(chat)`
- Asks follow-up questions in interactive mode - `chat.app()`.

# Generate a Claude Code Prompt

```{python}
generate_prompt_message = f"""
I like this plan. Please generate the prompt I will pass to Claude Code to execute it. Use the following template exactly, filling in all placeholders based on the lesson plan you just produced:

<template>
I have a Quarto lesson file that needs to be split into multiple shorter lessons. Please read the original file in full before doing anything else, then follow the steps below.

**Step 1: Read the original file**
Read the file at `{lesson_file_path}` in its entirety before proceeding.

**Step 2: Review the lesson plan**
Split the original lesson into the following new lessons:

| # | Folder Name | Filename |
|:-:|:------------|:---------|
| 1 | `[01 Lesson One Title]` | `[01 Lesson One Title.qmd]` |
| 2 | `[02 Lesson Two Title]` | `[02 Lesson Two Title.qmd]` |
| 3 | `[03 Lesson Three Title]` | `[03 Lesson Three Title.qmd]` |

Add or remove rows as needed. The content to include in each lesson is described below:

- **Lesson 1 – [Lesson One Title]:** [Describe the content to include]
- **Lesson 2 – [Lesson Two Title]:** [Describe the content to include]
- **Lesson 3 – [Lesson Three Title]:** [Describe the content to include]

**Step 3: Create the new lesson folders and files**
For each new lesson:
- Create a new subfolder inside `{lesson_folder_path}`, named exactly as shown in the table in Step 2.
- Inside each folder, create a `.qmd` file with the same name as the folder.
- Copy the YAML front matter from the original lesson into each new file, updating the `title` field to match the new lesson title. Leave all other YAML fields unchanged.

**Step 4: Populate each file with content**
- Copy prose and code from the original lesson into the appropriate new files verbatim. Do not rewrite, summarize, or paraphrase any content.
- Each new file should begin with the `library()` calls needed for the code in that lesson. Only include libraries that are actually used in that lesson's content.

**Step 5: Add simulation code**
- For each new lesson, identify which simulated datasets are actually used in that lesson's content.
- Copy only the relevant simulation code chunks from the original file.
- Place them after the `library()` calls but before the first instructional content.
- Add a brief one- or two-sentence prose introduction before each simulation block explaining that the data is being created for use in the lesson.

**Step 6: Review and flag**
After creating all files, provide a summary listing every folder and file created. Flag any code chunks that reference a dataset or object not defined within that same lesson file — these will need manual review.
</template>

Replace every bracketed placeholder in the template with the actual titles, folder names, filenames, and content descriptions from the lesson plan above. Do not change anything else in the template.
"""

chat.chat(generate_prompt_message)
```

Copy and paste the prompt into Claude Code.

# Save Chat History

See [Saving and Resuming Chats](https://brad-cannell.github.io/my-ai-assistant/vignettes/save-and-resume-chats.html) for the full workflow. Save both formats: markdown for browsing, pickle for resuming.

```{python}
# Markdown — for browsing and searching
chat_history_path_md = conversation_folder_path / ("chat_history_" + today + ".md")
chat.export(chat_history_path_md, overwrite=True)

# Pickle — for resuming in a later session
chat_history_path_pkl = conversation_folder_path / ("chat_history_" + today + ".pkl")
with open(chat_history_path_pkl, "wb") as f:
    pickle.dump(chat.get_turns(), f)
```
